title: Turner Syndrome
oneLiner: A chromosomal disorder in females caused by complete or partial loss of an X chromosome, leading to short stature, ovarian dysgenesis, and characteristic physical features.

sections.Turner Syndrome (45,X): **One-liner:** A chromosomal disorder in females caused by complete or partial loss of an X chromosome, leading to short stature, ovarian dysgenesis, and characteristic physical features.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette (ward/stem):**  
A 14-year-old girl is referred for short stature. She has been tracking below the 3rd centile since age 5. On examination, you note a low posterior hairline, webbed neck, widely spaced nipples, and cubitus valgus. She has not yet started puberty. Her school reports good academic performance but some difficulty with social cues. Growth velocity is 2 cm/year (well below normal for age). Parents are concerned about her short height and lack of breast development.

**Dissect the vignette:**

-   **Short stature (below 3rd centile)** – a hallmark; height is the most consistent feature, often present from early childhood.
-   **Low growth velocity (2 cm/year)** – indicates growth hormone axis involvement; typical slow linear growth.
-   **Lack of puberty (no breast development by 14)** – ovarian failure due to gonadal dysgenesis; streak gonads.
-   **Low posterior hairline + webbed neck** – lymphedema-related soft tissue excess in utero; now residual skin folds.
-   **Widely spaced nipples** – broad chest, another lymphatic / skeletal feature.
-   **Cubitus valgus** – increased carrying angle at elbow; associated skeletal anomaly.
-   **Good academics but social difficulties** – often normal intelligence but specific weaknesses in spatial/perceptual skills and social cognition.
-   **Family concerned about height** – typical prompt for referral.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

-   **Adult with primary amenorrhea + short stature** – the diagnosis may be missed in childhood if height only mildly affected. Pubertal failure is the presentation.
-   **Neonatal lymphoedema of hands/feet** – a newborn with puffy dorsum of feet, resolving spontaneously. This may be the first clue in a well baby.
-   **Recurrent otitis media or hearing loss in a short-statured girl** – high arched palate and Eustachian tube dysfunction are common; may be investigated as ENT problem first.
-   **Hypertension discovered at school screening** – coarctation of aorta is associated. Check pulses and BP in all short girls.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Turner syndrome is a condition affecting females where one of the two X chromosomes is completely or partly missing. This results in a spectrum of physical features, most notably short stature and failure of the ovaries to develop normally, leading to infertility.

**Why this matters clinically:**  
Turner syndrome affects 1 in 2000–2500 live-born females. It is one of the commonest chromosomal disorders. Many cases go undiagnosed until adolescence when puberty fails. Missing the diagnosis means missed opportunities for growth hormone therapy, oestrogen replacement, and surveillance for life-threatening complications like aortic dissection, type 2 diabetes, and osteoporosis. The vast majority of 45,X conceptuses miscarry – those born represent a less severe form, but still carry significant morbidity.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of development:**  
The missing or abnormal X chromosome (most commonly paternal loss in 45,X) leads to haploinsufficiency of pseudoautosomal genes that escape X-inactivation. Two key genes are *SHOX* (short stature homeobox) and *RPS6KA3* (RSK2). *SHOX* deficiency directly impairs linear bone growth, explaining the short stature, cubitus valgus, and Madelung deformity (wrist). Ovarian maintenance requires two active X chromosomes – the missing X causes accelerated follicular atresia in fetal life, leaving streak gonads (ovarian dysgenesis) by adolescence. Lymphatic development is disturbed, leading to fetal lymphedema that resolves in utero but leaves residual webbed neck, low hairline, and nail hypoplasia.

**Key signs and their mechanisms:**

| Clinical sign | Mechanism |
| --- | --- |
| Short stature | Haploinsufficiency of *SHOX* – impaired chondrocyte proliferation in growth plates; also modest GH resistance |
| Webbed neck, low hairline, broad chest | In utero lymphatic congestion (left-sided thoracic duct obstruction) resolving with fibrosis |
| Cubitus valgus, Madelung deformity | *SHOX* deficiency → radial and ulnar skeletal dysplasia |
| Ovarian dysgenesis | Accelerated germ cell apoptosis due to lack of second X; streak ovaries |
| Sensorineural hearing loss | Abnormal development of cochlear structures; also recurrent middle ear effusions from Eustachian tube dysfunction |
| Coarctation of aorta / bicuspid aortic valve | Defective left-sided heart development; possible *FOXF2* / vascular remodelling gene interaction |
| Renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney) | Disrupted metanephric development |
| Normal intelligence but social deficits | X-chromosome genes involved in spatial processing and social cognition; partial functional disomy |
| Oestrogen deficiency → osteoporosis, delayed puberty | No ovarian hormone production from streak gonads |

**Connecting back to pattern recognition:**  
The short stature + pubertal failure + typical physical features all stem from one root: X-chromosome monosomy. The webbed neck and cubitus valgus are legacy signs of fetal lymphedema and *SHOX* deficiency. Normal intelligence with social challenges distinguishes Turner from other short-stature syndromes.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**Most common cause:** Complete loss of paternal X chromosome (45,X) – accounts for ~80% of cases. There is no known environmental trigger; this is usually a random error in paternal meiosis or early post-zygotic mitosis.

**Causes by category:**

| Category | Cause | 1-line stem clue |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Genetic (sporadic) | Complete monosomy X (45,X) | Classic presentation with all features; often the paternal X missing |
| Genetic (sporadic) | Isochromosome Xq (46,X,i(Xq)) – long arm duplication, short arm loss | Milder phenotype; may have some ovarian function |
| Genetic (mosaic) | 45,X/46,XX | Very mild – may be fertile, shorter than family but not striking; often missed |
| Genetic (mosaic) | 45,X/46,XY | Mixed phenotype; may have virilisation or ambiguous genitalia; risk of gonadoblastoma |
| Genetic | Ring X (small ring chromosome) | May have more intellectual disability if ring disrupts X-inactivation centre |
| Genetic | De novo deletion of *SHOX* gene (PTH area on Xp) | Not truly Turner; presents as Léri-Weill dyschondrosteosis – short stature + Madelung deformity alone |

**Key teaching point:** No infectious, autoimmune, or iatrogenic causes. It is purely genetic; occurs de novo. Parental karyotypes are normal.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
Turner syndrome is diagnosed by karyotype. The diagnosis can be suspected prenatally on ultrasound (cystic hygroma, hydrops, coarctation). Postnatally, any female with short stature plus any of: webbed neck, pubertal delay, coarctation, or characteristic dysmorphism should have a karyotype.

**Test-by-test breakdown:**

| Test | What it shows | Why we order it | How to interpret |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Karyotype (peripheral blood) | Chromosome number and structure | Confirms diagnosis; identifies monosomy, isochromosome, mosaic, or ring | 45,X = classic; 46,X,i(Xq) = isochromosome; 45,X/46,XX = mosaic (low abnormal line %); 45,X/46,XY = high gonadoblastoma risk |
| FISH (rapid X-centromere probe) | Detects X monosomy in interphase cells | Quick screen from blood or buccal smear | Useful if urgent result needed or poor blood culture |
| Echocardiogram | Cardiac anatomy (aortic valve, arch) | All newly diagnosed Turner patients need imaging to detect coarctation / bicuspid valve | Bicuspid aortic valve (30%) may need prophylaxis; coarctation needs surgical repair |
| Renal ultrasound | Kidney structure and anatomy | Detects horseshoe kidney, duplication, malrotation | Horseshoe kidney present in 10–15%; may cause UTIs, hypertension |
| TSH, free T4 | Thyroid function | Autoimmune hypothyroidism common (up to 50% lifetime risk) | Elevated TSH + low T4 = start levothyroxine |
| Celiac screen (tTG IgA + total IgA) | Antibodies for celiac disease | Increased prevalence (5–10%) | If positive → refer for endoscopy; treat with gluten-free diet |
| LH, FSH, oestradiol | Pubertal status | Elevated FSH (often >40 IU/L) by age 10 indicates gonadal failure | Low oestradiol + high FSH = streak gonads; need oestrogen replacement |
| Growth hormone stimulation test | Confirms GH deficiency? | Not always needed if clinical + karyotype; but if considering GH therapy, some guidelines suggest testing GH axis | Peak GH <10 ng/mL = deficiency; but most have normal GH levels, just *SHOX* dysfunction |
| Bone age (left hand/wrist X-ray) | Skeletal maturity | To guide growth hormone therapy and predict adult height | Bone age usually delayed 2–3 years in adolescence |
| Audiogram | Hearing threshold | Baseline screening; repeat every 5 years | Sensorineural high-frequency loss; also conductive from otitis media |

**Single confirming finding:** Karyotype showing 45,X or 45,X/46,XX in a phenotypic female. If mosaicism is low (<5% of cells), consider a second tissue (skin biopsy) if high suspicion.

**Pattern recognition: the one that rules out**  
If a short female has a normal 46,XX karyotype, it is NOT Turner syndrome – consider Noonan syndrome or other short stature causes.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour – recognition of crisis):**  
Acute aortic dissection is rare but catastrophic in Turner. If a patient presents with tearing chest pain, unequal arm BP, widened mediastinum → **CT aortogram, call cardiothoracic team, start IV labetalol (10–20 mg IV over 2 min, repeat after 10–30 min, titrate to heart rate <60, SBP 100–120 mmHg)**. This is not common, but you must have high index in any Turner patient with hypertension.

**Ward (24–72h – initial workup):**

-   **Growth hormone therapy:** Start as soon as diagnosis confirmed – usually age 5–6.  
    *Dose:* Somatropin 0.05 mg/kg/day SC once daily.  
    *Why:* Improves final adult height by ~5–8 cm on average.  
    *Monitor:* Height velocity, bone age, IGF-1 (keep <+2SD).  
    *Caution:* Monitor for raised intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri) – headache, visual changes – stop GH if occurs.

-   **Oestrogen replacement:** Induce puberty around age 11–12 (bone age 11–12).  
    *Dose:* Ethinylestradiol 2–5 mcg daily PO (or 17β-oestradiol transdermal 6.25–12.5 mcg/24h patch). Titrate over 2–3 years.  
    *Why:* Promote secondary sexual characteristics, bone mineralisation, and psychosocial wellbeing.  
    *Caution:* Starting too early – shortens growth. Starting too late – risk of osteoporosis and poor bone mass accrual.

-   **Progestogen:** Add once breakthrough bleeding or after 2 years of oestrogen, to induce cyclic withdrawal.  
    *Dose:* Norethisterone 5 mg daily for 12–14 days per month or dydrogesterone 10 mg daily for 14 days.  
    *Why:* Reduce endometrial hyperplasia risk.

-   **Coarctation management:** If present, refer to paediatric cardiology and cardiac surgery. Usually requires resection / angioplasty in infancy or childhood. BP monitoring lifelong.

-   **Thyroid screening:** Check TSH yearly.

**Long-term (adult care):**

-   **Oestrogen continuation:** Usually until age 50 (average menopause). Consider tibolone (2.5 mg OD) as alternative with less bleeding.
-   **Fertility options:** Most are infertile. Discuss oocyte donation / IVF with donor egg. For women with mosaic 45,X/46,XX, some may have spontaneous ovarian function.
-   **Osteoporosis prevention:** Ensure calcium 1000 mg/day + vitamin D 800 IU/day. DEXA scan every 2–5 years.
-   **Cardiovascular surveillance:** Yearly echo for aortic root size. If root > 35 mm or rapid growth, refer to cardiology. Strict BP control (<130/80).
-   **Hearing aid** if sensorineural loss.
-   **Social support** – learning support for nonverbal skills; consider referral to psychologist.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

-   **Do not** start oestrogen before bone age 11-12 – you will stunt final height.
-   **Do not** stop growth hormone arbitrarily – continue until bone age >14 girls or height satisfactory.
-   **Do not** forget that Turner patients require anaesthesia evaluation for difficult intubation (short neck, webbing).
-   **Do not** miss coarctation – always measure BP in all four limbs.
-   **Do not** assume infertility – some mosaics may conceive, so counsel accordingly.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Turner Syndrome (45,X) | Noonan Syndrome | Mixed Gonadal Dysgenesis (45,X/46,XY) | Silver-Russell Syndrome |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Cause** | Monosomy X | *PTPN11, SOS1, RAF1* mutations | Mosaic X/XY | 11p15 hypomethylation, maternal UPD7 |
| **Karyotype** | 45,X or variant | 46,XX (normal) | 45,X/46,XY | Normal 46,XX |
| **Height** | Short, <3rd centile | Short, <3rd centile | Short | Severe pre- and postnatal short stature, relative macrocephaly |
| **Webbed neck** | Yes (classic) | Yes (similar, but more prominent) | Possible | No |
| **Cubitus valgus** | Yes | Yes | Possible | No |
| **Gonads** | Streak ovaries, no puberty | Normal (may have delayed puberty if cryptorchid) | Streak/ovotestes; ambiguous genitalia | Normal |
| **Cardiac** | Coarctation, bicuspid aortic valve | Pulmonary stenosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | Coarctation possible | No |
| **Chest shape** | Broad, widely spaced nipples | Pectus carinatum/excavatum | Variable | Asymmetric lower limbs |
| **Intellectual disability** | Normal IQ, social/visuospatial deficits | Mild ID often present | Variable | Normal; feeding difficulties prominent |
| **Hearing** | Sensorineural loss, otitis media | Sensorineural loss possible | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differing feature** | Karyotype confirms; Webbed neck + short stature + streak gonads | Normal karyotype; genetic testing for RAS-MAPK pathway; characteristic facies | Karyotype with Y material; ambiguous genitalia | Asymmetric body; prominent forehead; triangular face |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complication 1: Aortic dissection / rupture**  
- **Presentation:** Acute severe tearing chest or back pain, syncope, unequal BP, pulse deficits, new aortic regurgitation murmur. High index – rising can be the first event.  
- **Key signs:** Hypertension (often undiagnosed), widened mediastinum on CXR, positive CT aortogram.  
- **What to do:** Emergency surgery. While awaiting: IV labetalol (as above) or esmolol, avoid nitroprusside without beta-blockade (can increase wall stress).  
- **Late presentation if untreated:** Sudden death.

**Major complication 2: Osteoporosis with fragility fractures**  
- **Presentation:** Wrist, hip, or vertebral compression fracture in a woman aged 40–50.  
- **Key signs:** Low BMD on DEXA. Often low oestradiol (if not on HRT).  
- **What to do:** Start / optimise oestrogen + calcium/vitamin D. Consider bisphosphonate if established osteoporosis.  
- **If missed:** Progressive kyphosis, loss of height, multiple fractures.

**Major complication 3: Type 2 diabetes mellitus**  
- **Presentation:** Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss – or incidentally detected on screening.  
- **Key signs:** Increased body mass index in many; insulin resistance is common even without obesity.  
- **What to do:** Screen HbA1c yearly. Treat metformin first line (500–850 mg PO BID, titrate to 2 g daily).  
- **If missed:** Cardiovascular disease, nephropathy, retinopathy.

**Major complication 4: Hypertension (often from coarctation or renal anomaly)**  
- **Presentation:** Asymptomatic on school screening; or headache, nosebleeds.  
- **Key signs:** BP >130/80 consistently. Check four-limb BP – if leg BP lower than arm by >20 mmHg, suspect coarctation.  
- **What to do:** Echo to assess coarctation; if normal, treat as essential hypertension with ACEi/ARB (e.g., enalapril 5–20 mg OD).  
- **If missed:** Left ventricular hypertrophy, aortic root dilation, stroke, dissection.

**Late-presentation patterns in exam stems:**  
- “A 45-year-old woman with short stature (4’10”) and primary amenorrhea presents with chest pain” → Dissection.  
- “A 12-year-old girl with no pubertal development” → Ovarian failure.  
- “A neonate with puffy feet” → Lymphoedema → check karyotype.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

-   **“TURNER”** – Teach the features:  
    T = Tall stature? No, **Short**  
    U = **U**ndergrown ovaries  
    R = **R**enal anomalies (horseshoe)  
    N = **N**ipples widely spaced, **N**o puberty, **N**eck webbed  
    E = **E**strogen deficiency  
    R = **R**adius deviation (cubitus valgus)

-   **“SHOX”** – Gene involved:  
    S = Short stature  
    H = Hereditary? No, sporadic  
    O = Ovarian dysgenesis  
    X = X chromosome

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1.  **“The short girl with the wide neck”** – always get karyotype. Even if webbing is subtle, a low hairline is enough.

2.  **“Hearing loss + short stature in a girl”** – turner until proven otherwise.

3.  **“Neonatal lymphoedema of the feet that resolves”** – this is a classic clue; document it.

4.  **“Primary amenorrhea + short stature”** – the two most common presenting complaints after age 14.

5.  **“Coarctation of aorta in a girl”** – must consider Turner; measure arm/leg BP.

6.  **“Normal intelligence but poor social skills / math difficulty”** – characteristic cognitive profile.

7.  **“Oestrogen replacement is key for bone health”** – never leave adult Turner patients without HRT unless contraindicated.

8.  **“Watch for thyroid and celiac autoimmunity”** – screen annually.

9.  **“Aortic root measurement with echo – if >35 mm, involve cardio”** – risk of dissection is 6x higher.

10. **“Infertility is not absolute”** – in mosaics (45,X/46,XX) some have spontaneous menarche. Counsel individually.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**

-   Confusing Turner with Noonan (Noonan has normal karyotype, pulmonary stenosis, typically mild ID).
-   Starting oestrogen too early – stunts growth.
-   Ignoring hypertension – leads to dissection.
-   Forgetting to check for Y-chromosome material in 45,X/46,XY mosaic (risk of gonadoblastoma) – remove gonads.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**  
- **Suspected aortic dissection** – immediate, any age.  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** – due to chest pain, hypotension, dyspnoea.  
- **GCS < 15** – from dissection, stroke, pseudotumor cerebri.  
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – consider dissection causing mesenteric ischaemia.  
- **Hypertensive emergency (BP >180/120)** – if untreated coarctation or renovascular cause.  
- **Severe unexplained headache + vision changes** – suspect pseudotumor cerebri from GH therapy. Stop GH and refer neurosurg/ophthal.

**Specific thresholds:**  
- **BP** consistently >140/90 in child or >130/80 in adult → escalate to endocrinology / cardiology.  
- **Aortic root > 35 mm** (or z-score >2 in children) → refer to adult congenital heart disease specialist.  
- **Growth rate < 2 cm/year** despite GH → discuss with specialist team; consider GH dose adjustment or alternative diagnoses.  
- **Oestradiol level** – if <10 pmol/L on full dose after 2 years → suspect non-compliance or absorption issue; consider IM route.

**When to call crash team:**  
- Sudden chest pain with haemodynamic instability.  
- Seizure with raised ICP signs.  
- Cardiac arrest – likely from dissection, arrhythmia, or electrolyte imbalance (rare, but watch for hypoglycaemia in GH therapy).

## Turner Syndrome (45,X)

**One-liner:** A chromosomal disorder in females caused by complete or partial loss of an X chromosome, leading to short stature, ovarian dysgenesis, and characteristic physical features.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette (ward/stem):**  
A 14-year-old girl is referred for short stature. She has been tracking below the 3rd centile since age 5. On examination, you note a low posterior hairline, webbed neck, widely spaced nipples, and cubitus valgus. She has not yet started puberty. Her school reports good academic performance but some difficulty with social cues. Growth velocity is 2 cm/year (well below normal for age). Parents are concerned about her short height and lack of breast development.

**Dissect the vignette:**

-   **Short stature (below 3rd centile)** – a hallmark; height is the most consistent feature, often present from early childhood.
-   **Low growth velocity (2 cm/year)** – indicates growth hormone axis involvement; typical slow linear growth.
-   **Lack of puberty (no breast development by 14)** – ovarian failure due to gonadal dysgenesis; streak gonads.
-   **Low posterior hairline + webbed neck** – lymphedema-related soft tissue excess in utero; now residual skin folds.
-   **Widely spaced nipples** – broad chest, another lymphatic / skeletal feature.
-   **Cubitus valgus** – increased carrying angle at elbow; associated skeletal anomaly.
-   **Good academics but social difficulties** – often normal intelligence but specific weaknesses in spatial/perceptual skills and social cognition.
-   **Family concerned about height** – typical prompt for referral.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

-   **Adult with primary amenorrhea + short stature** – the diagnosis may be missed in childhood if height only mildly affected. Pubertal failure is the presentation.
-   **Neonatal lymphoedema of hands/feet** – a newborn with puffy dorsum of feet, resolving spontaneously. This may be the first clue in a well baby.
-   **Recurrent otitis media or hearing loss in a short-statured girl** – high arched palate and Eustachian tube dysfunction are common; may be investigated as ENT problem first.
-   **Hypertension discovered at school screening** – coarctation of aorta is associated. Check pulses and BP in all short girls.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Turner syndrome is a condition affecting females where one of the two X chromosomes is completely or partly missing. This results in a spectrum of physical features, most notably short stature and failure of the ovaries to develop normally, leading to infertility.

**Why this matters clinically:**  
Turner syndrome affects 1 in 2000–2500 live-born females. It is one of the commonest chromosomal disorders. Many cases go undiagnosed until adolescence when puberty fails. Missing the diagnosis means missed opportunities for growth hormone therapy, oestrogen replacement, and surveillance for life-threatening complications like aortic dissection, type 2 diabetes, and osteoporosis. The vast majority of 45,X conceptuses miscarry – those born represent a less severe form, but still carry significant morbidity.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of development:**  
The missing or abnormal X chromosome (most commonly paternal loss in 45,X) leads to haploinsufficiency of pseudoautosomal genes that escape X-inactivation. Two key genes are *SHOX* (short stature homeobox) and *RPS6KA3* (RSK2). *SHOX* deficiency directly impairs linear bone growth, explaining the short stature, cubitus valgus, and Madelung deformity (wrist). Ovarian maintenance requires two active X chromosomes – the missing X causes accelerated follicular atresia in fetal life, leaving streak gonads (ovarian dysgenesis) by adolescence. Lymphatic development is disturbed, leading to fetal lymphedema that resolves in utero but leaves residual webbed neck, low hairline, and nail hypoplasia.

**Key signs and their mechanisms:**

| Clinical sign | Mechanism |
| --- | --- |
| Short stature | Haploinsufficiency of *SHOX* – impaired chondrocyte proliferation in growth plates; also modest GH resistance |
| Webbed neck, low hairline, broad chest | In utero lymphatic congestion (left-sided thoracic duct obstruction) resolving with fibrosis |
| Cubitus valgus, Madelung deformity | *SHOX* deficiency → radial and ulnar skeletal dysplasia |
| Ovarian dysgenesis | Accelerated germ cell apoptosis due to lack of second X; streak ovaries |
| Sensorineural hearing loss | Abnormal development of cochlear structures; also recurrent middle ear effusions from Eustachian tube dysfunction |
| Coarctation of aorta / bicuspid aortic valve | Defective left-sided heart development; possible *FOXF2* / vascular remodelling gene interaction |
| Renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney) | Disrupted metanephric development |
| Normal intelligence but social deficits | X-chromosome genes involved in spatial processing and social cognition; partial functional disomy |
| Oestrogen deficiency → osteoporosis, delayed puberty | No ovarian hormone production from streak gonads |

**Connecting back to pattern recognition:**  
The short stature + pubertal failure + typical physical features all stem from one root: X-chromosome monosomy. The webbed neck and cubitus valgus are legacy signs of fetal lymphedema and *SHOX* deficiency. Normal intelligence with social challenges distinguishes Turner from other short-stature syndromes.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**Most common cause:** Complete loss of paternal X chromosome (45,X) – accounts for ~80% of cases. There is no known environmental trigger; this is usually a random error in paternal meiosis or early post-zygotic mitosis.

**Causes by category:**

| Category | Cause | 1-line stem clue |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Genetic (sporadic) | Complete monosomy X (45,X) | Classic presentation with all features; often the paternal X missing |
| Genetic (sporadic) | Isochromosome Xq (46,X,i(Xq)) – long arm duplication, short arm loss | Milder phenotype; may have some ovarian function |
| Genetic (mosaic) | 45,X/46,XX | Very mild – may be fertile, shorter than family but not striking; often missed |
| Genetic (mosaic) | 45,X/46,XY | Mixed phenotype; may have virilisation or ambiguous genitalia; risk of gonadoblastoma |
| Genetic | Ring X (small ring chromosome) | May have more intellectual disability if ring disrupts X-inactivation centre |
| Genetic | De novo deletion of *SHOX* gene (PTH area on Xp) | Not truly Turner; presents as Léri-Weill dyschondrosteosis – short stature + Madelung deformity alone |

**Key teaching point:** No infectious, autoimmune, or iatrogenic causes. It is purely genetic; occurs de novo. Parental karyotypes are normal.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
Turner syndrome is diagnosed by karyotype. The diagnosis can be suspected prenatally on ultrasound (cystic hygroma, hydrops, coarctation). Postnatally, any female with short stature plus any of: webbed neck, pubertal delay, coarctation, or characteristic dysmorphism should have a karyotype.

**Test-by-test breakdown:**

| Test | What it shows | Why we order it | How to interpret |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Karyotype (peripheral blood) | Chromosome number and structure | Confirms diagnosis; identifies monosomy, isochromosome, mosaic, or ring | 45,X = classic; 46,X,i(Xq) = isochromosome; 45,X/46,XX = mosaic (low abnormal line %); 45,X/46,XY = high gonadoblastoma risk |
| FISH (rapid X-centromere probe) | Detects X monosomy in interphase cells | Quick screen from blood or buccal smear | Useful if urgent result needed or poor blood culture |
| Echocardiogram | Cardiac anatomy (aortic valve, arch) | All newly diagnosed Turner patients need imaging to detect coarctation / bicuspid valve | Bicuspid aortic valve (30%) may need prophylaxis; coarctation needs surgical repair |
| Renal ultrasound | Kidney structure and anatomy | Detects horseshoe kidney, duplication, malrotation | Horseshoe kidney present in 10–15%; may cause UTIs, hypertension |
| TSH, free T4 | Thyroid function | Autoimmune hypothyroidism common (up to 50% lifetime risk) | Elevated TSH + low T4 = start levothyroxine |
| Celiac screen (tTG IgA + total IgA) | Antibodies for celiac disease | Increased prevalence (5–10%) | If positive → refer for endoscopy; treat with gluten-free diet |
| LH, FSH, oestradiol | Pubertal status | Elevated FSH (often >40 IU/L) by age 10 indicates gonadal failure | Low oestradiol + high FSH = streak gonads; need oestrogen replacement |
| Growth hormone stimulation test | Confirms GH deficiency? | Not always needed if clinical + karyotype; but if considering GH therapy, some guidelines suggest testing GH axis | Peak GH <10 ng/mL = deficiency; but most have normal GH levels, just *SHOX* dysfunction |
| Bone age (left hand/wrist X-ray) | Skeletal maturity | To guide growth hormone therapy and predict adult height | Bone age usually delayed 2–3 years in adolescence |
| Audiogram | Hearing threshold | Baseline screening; repeat every 5 years | Sensorineural high-frequency loss; also conductive from otitis media |

**Single confirming finding:** Karyotype showing 45,X or 45,X/46,XX in a phenotypic female. If mosaicism is low (<5% of cells), consider a second tissue (skin biopsy) if high suspicion.

**Pattern recognition: the one that rules out**  
If a short female has a normal 46,XX karyotype, it is NOT Turner syndrome – consider Noonan syndrome or other short stature causes.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour – recognition of crisis):**  
Acute aortic dissection is rare but catastrophic in Turner. If a patient presents with tearing chest pain, unequal arm BP, widened mediastinum → **CT aortogram, call cardiothoracic team, start IV labetalol (10–20 mg IV over 2 min, repeat after 10–30 min, titrate to heart rate <60, SBP 100–120 mmHg)**. This is not common, but you must have high index in any Turner patient with hypertension.

**Ward (24–72h – initial workup):**

-   **Growth hormone therapy:** Start as soon as diagnosis confirmed – usually age 5–6.  
    *Dose:* Somatropin 0.05 mg/kg/day SC once daily.  
    *Why:* Improves final adult height by ~5–8 cm on average.  
    *Monitor:* Height velocity, bone age, IGF-1 (keep <+2SD).  
    *Caution:* Monitor for raised intracranial pressure (pseudotumor cerebri) – headache, visual changes – stop GH if occurs.

-   **Oestrogen replacement:** Induce puberty around age 11–12 (bone age 11–12).  
    *Dose:* Ethinylestradiol 2–5 mcg daily PO (or 17β-oestradiol transdermal 6.25–12.5 mcg/24h patch). Titrate over 2–3 years.  
    *Why:* Promote secondary sexual characteristics, bone mineralisation, and psychosocial wellbeing.  
    *Caution:* Starting too early – shortens growth. Starting too late – risk of osteoporosis and poor bone mass accrual.

-   **Progestogen:** Add once breakthrough bleeding or after 2 years of oestrogen, to induce cyclic withdrawal.  
    *Dose:* Norethisterone 5 mg daily for 12–14 days per month or dydrogesterone 10 mg daily for 14 days.  
    *Why:* Reduce endometrial hyperplasia risk.

-   **Coarctation management:** If present, refer to paediatric cardiology and cardiac surgery. Usually requires resection / angioplasty in infancy or childhood. BP monitoring lifelong.

-   **Thyroid screening:** Check TSH yearly.

**Long-term (adult care):**

-   **Oestrogen continuation:** Usually until age 50 (average menopause). Consider tibolone (2.5 mg OD) as alternative with less bleeding.
-   **Fertility options:** Most are infertile. Discuss oocyte donation / IVF with donor egg. For women with mosaic 45,X/46,XX, some may have spontaneous ovarian function.
-   **Osteoporosis prevention:** Ensure calcium 1000 mg/day + vitamin D 800 IU/day. DEXA scan every 2–5 years.
-   **Cardiovascular surveillance:** Yearly echo for aortic root size. If root > 35 mm or rapid growth, refer to cardiology. Strict BP control (<130/80).
-   **Hearing aid** if sensorineural loss.
-   **Social support** – learning support for nonverbal skills; consider referral to psychologist.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**

-   **Do not** start oestrogen before bone age 11-12 – you will stunt final height.
-   **Do not** stop growth hormone arbitrarily – continue until bone age >14 girls or height satisfactory.
-   **Do not** forget that Turner patients require anaesthesia evaluation for difficult intubation (short neck, webbing).
-   **Do not** miss coarctation – always measure BP in all four limbs.
-   **Do not** assume infertility – some mosaics may conceive, so counsel accordingly.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Turner Syndrome (45,X) | Noonan Syndrome | Mixed Gonadal Dysgenesis (45,X/46,XY) | Silver-Russell Syndrome |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Cause** | Monosomy X | *PTPN11, SOS1, RAF1* mutations | Mosaic X/XY | 11p15 hypomethylation, maternal UPD7 |
| **Karyotype** | 45,X or variant | 46,XX (normal) | 45,X/46,XY | Normal 46,XX |
| **Height** | Short, <3rd centile | Short, <3rd centile | Short | Severe pre- and postnatal short stature, relative macrocephaly |
| **Webbed neck** | Yes (classic) | Yes (similar, but more prominent) | Possible | No |
| **Cubitus valgus** | Yes | Yes | Possible | No |
| **Gonads** | Streak ovaries, no puberty | Normal (may have delayed puberty if cryptorchid) | Streak/ovotestes; ambiguous genitalia | Normal |
| **Cardiac** | Coarctation, bicuspid aortic valve | Pulmonary stenosis, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy | Coarctation possible | No |
| **Chest shape** | Broad, widely spaced nipples | Pectus carinatum/excavatum | Variable | Asymmetric lower limbs |
| **Intellectual disability** | Normal IQ, social/visuospatial deficits | Mild ID often present | Variable | Normal; feeding difficulties prominent |
| **Hearing** | Sensorineural loss, otitis media | Sensorineural loss possible | Normal | Normal |
| **Key differing feature** | Karyotype confirms; Webbed neck + short stature + streak gonads | Normal karyotype; genetic testing for RAS-MAPK pathway; characteristic facies | Karyotype with Y material; ambiguous genitalia | Asymmetric body; prominent forehead; triangular face |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complication 1: Aortic dissection / rupture**  
- **Presentation:** Acute severe tearing chest or back pain, syncope, unequal BP, pulse deficits, new aortic regurgitation murmur. High index – rising can be the first event.  
- **Key signs:** Hypertension (often undiagnosed), widened mediastinum on CXR, positive CT aortogram.  
- **What to do:** Emergency surgery. While awaiting: IV labetalol (as above) or esmolol, avoid nitroprusside without beta-blockade (can increase wall stress).  
- **Late presentation if untreated:** Sudden death.

**Major complication 2: Osteoporosis with fragility fractures**  
- **Presentation:** Wrist, hip, or vertebral compression fracture in a woman aged 40–50.  
- **Key signs:** Low BMD on DEXA. Often low oestradiol (if not on HRT).  
- **What to do:** Start / optimise oestrogen + calcium/vitamin D. Consider bisphosphonate if established osteoporosis.  
- **If missed:** Progressive kyphosis, loss of height, multiple fractures.

**Major complication 3: Type 2 diabetes mellitus**  
- **Presentation:** Polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss – or incidentally detected on screening.  
- **Key signs:** Increased body mass index in many; insulin resistance is common even without obesity.  
- **What to do:** Screen HbA1c yearly. Treat metformin first line (500–850 mg PO BID, titrate to 2 g daily).  
- **If missed:** Cardiovascular disease, nephropathy, retinopathy.

**Major complication 4: Hypertension (often from coarctation or renal anomaly)**  
- **Presentation:** Asymptomatic on school screening; or headache, nosebleeds.  
- **Key signs:** BP >130/80 consistently. Check four-limb BP – if leg BP lower than arm by >20 mmHg, suspect coarctation.  
- **What to do:** Echo to assess coarctation; if normal, treat as essential hypertension with ACEi/ARB (e.g., enalapril 5–20 mg OD).  
- **If missed:** Left ventricular hypertrophy, aortic root dilation, stroke, dissection.

**Late-presentation patterns in exam stems:**  
- “A 45-year-old woman with short stature (4’10”) and primary amenorrhea presents with chest pain” → Dissection.  
- “A 12-year-old girl with no pubertal development” → Ovarian failure.  
- “A neonate with puffy feet” → Lymphoedema → check karyotype.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

-   **“TURNER”** – Teach the features:  
    T = Tall stature? No, **Short**  
    U = **U**ndergrown ovaries  
    R = **R**enal anomalies (horseshoe)  
    N = **N**ipples widely spaced, **N**o puberty, **N**eck webbed  
    E = **E**strogen deficiency  
    R = **R**adius deviation (cubitus valgus)

-   **“SHOX”** – Gene involved:  
    S = Short stature  
    H = Hereditary? No, sporadic  
    O = Ovarian dysgenesis  
    X = X chromosome

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**

1.  **“The short girl with the wide neck”** – always get karyotype. Even if webbing is subtle, a low hairline is enough.

2.  **“Hearing loss + short stature in a girl”** – turner until proven otherwise.

3.  **“Neonatal lymphoedema of the feet that resolves”** – this is a classic clue; document it.

4.  **“Primary amenorrhea + short stature”** – the two most common presenting complaints after age 14.

5.  **“Coarctation of aorta in a girl”** – must consider Turner; measure arm/leg BP.

6.  **“Normal intelligence but poor social skills / math difficulty”** – characteristic cognitive profile.

7.  **“Oestrogen replacement is key for bone health”** – never leave adult Turner patients without HRT unless contraindicated.

8.  **“Watch for thyroid and celiac autoimmunity”** – screen annually.

9.  **“Aortic root measurement with echo – if >35 mm, involve cardio”** – risk of dissection is 6x higher.

10. **“Infertility is not absolute”** – in mosaics (45,X/46,XX) some have spontaneous menarche. Counsel individually.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**

-   Confusing Turner with Noonan (Noonan has normal karyotype, pulmonary stenosis, typically mild ID).
-   Starting oestrogen too early – stunts growth.
-   Ignoring hypertension – leads to dissection.
-   Forgetting to check for Y-chromosome material in 45,X/46,XY mosaic (risk of gonadoblastoma) – remove gonads.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**  
- **Suspected aortic dissection** – immediate, any age.  
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** – due to chest pain, hypotension, dyspnoea.  
- **GCS < 15** – from dissection, stroke, pseudotumor cerebri.  
- **Lactate > 4 mmol/L** – consider dissection causing mesenteric ischaemia.  
- **Hypertensive emergency (BP >180/120)** – if untreated coarctation or renovascular cause.  
- **Severe unexplained headache + vision changes** – suspect pseudotumor cerebri from GH therapy. Stop GH and refer neurosurg/ophthal.

**Specific thresholds:**  
- **BP** consistently >140/90 in child or >130/80 in adult → escalate to endocrinology / cardiology.  
- **Aortic root > 35 mm** (or z-score >2 in children) → refer to adult congenital heart disease specialist.  
- **Growth rate < 2 cm/year** despite GH → discuss with specialist team; consider GH dose adjustment or alternative diagnoses.  
- **Oestradiol level** – if <10 pmol/L on full dose after 2 years → suspect non-compliance or absorption issue; consider IM route.

**When to call crash team:**  
- Sudden chest pain with haemodynamic instability.  
- Seizure with raised ICP signs.  
- Cardiac arrest – likely from dissection, arrhythmia, or electrolyte imbalance (rare, but watch for hypoglycaemia in GH therapy).
